did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) combined with a Premature Convergence Strategy-based Artificial Jelly Search Optimization (PCSAJSO) algorithm for skin cancer classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). It also employs a W-Net deep learning architecture for skin lesion segmentation. The methods were evaluated on dermatology datasets including ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018, ISIC2019, and PH2.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel 'Premature Convergence Strategy-based Artificial Jelly Search Optimization (PCSAJSO)' algorithm combined with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods (W-Net segmentation, hyperparameter tuning), describes experimental setups using datasets (ISIC2016-2019, PH2), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, precision, etc.) in tables, distinguishing it from a review paper which only summarizes prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Classification Using Premature Convergence Strategy-based Artificial Jelly Search Optimization with Convolutional Neural Network
journal: International Journal of Intelligent Engineering and Systems
brief_summary: This study proposes a skin cancer classification method combining a Premature Convergence Strategy-based Artificial Jelly Search Optimization (PCSAJSO) algorithm with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma lesions. The approach utilizes W-Net for skin lesion segmentation and applies pre-processing techniques like Gaussian filtering and Dull Razor to remove noise and hair interference. The model was evaluated on five dermatology datasets: ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018, ISIC2019, and PH2. Performance was assessed using metrics including accuracy, precision, specificity, sensitivity, Jaccard Index, and Dice coefficient. The proposed PCSAJSO-CNN achieved high accuracies of 98.15%, 99.38%, 99.70%, 94.68%, and 97.08% on the respective datasets. These results demonstrate that the method outperforms existing techniques such as Inception-ResNet and U-Net in classifying skin cancer.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma and non-melanoma categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'classifying skin cancer into melanoma and non-melanoma' using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) optimized by PCSAJSO. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, Precision, Specificity, and Sensitivity on datasets including ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018, ISIC2019, and PH2. While W-Net is used for segmentation (a secondary task), the primary prediction task reported is classification.

The paper utilizes a W-Net architecture for 'segmenting skin cancer by capturing the contextual information' and removing noise/hair. Segmentation performance is reported using Jaccard Index (JAC) and Dice coefficient metrics in Table 3.

ISIC2016.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Specificity, Sensitivity, JAC, DICE
ISIC2016.notes: The proposed method achieved superior accuracy compared to W-Net – Inception Resnet.

ISIC2017.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Specificity, Sensitivity, JAC, DICE
ISIC2017.notes: The proposed method outperformed RDCNN, FLM, and W-Net – Inception Resnet in accuracy.

ISIC2018.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Specificity, Sensitivity, JAC, DICE
ISIC2018.notes: The proposed method achieved the highest accuracy (99.70%) among compared methods.

ISIC2019.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Specificity, Sensitivity, JAC, DICE
ISIC2019.notes: The proposed method achieved higher accuracy, precision, and sensitivity compared to RDCNN.

PH2.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Specificity, Sensitivity, JAC, DICE
PH2.notes: The proposed method achieved higher accuracy and precision compared to W-Net – Inception Resnet.

Segmentation Results (W-Net).notes: W-Net consistently achieved the highest JAC and DICE scores across all datasets compared to ANN, CapNet, RNN, and DNN.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) optimized by Premature Convergence Strategy-based Artificial Jelly Search Optimization (PCSAJSO); W-Net for segmentation
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models ResNet-50 and VGG-16 were involved for feature extraction and adapted for global classification performance.

architecture_details.optimization_algorithm: PCSAJSO (Premature Convergence Strategy-based Artificial Jelly Search Optimization)
architecture_details.optimization_details: PCSAJSO combines Artificial Jelly Search Optimization (AJSO) with a Premature Convergence Strategy (PCS) and a chaotic logistic map. It uses Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for feature reduction. The AJSO involves three phases: ocean current navigation, food search, and staying at food locations. PCS adjusts population size and control parameters to balance exploration and exploitation.

training_details.hardware: i6 processor, 16 GB RAM, Windows 10 OS

preprocessing_techniques: Dull Razor method for hair removal
W-Net segmentation for lesion region identification and noise/hair elimination
conclusions: The proposed PCSAJSO-CNN method effectively classifies skin cancer into melanoma and non-melanoma categories, achieving high accuracy across multiple datasets.
The PCSAJSO algorithm successfully balances exploration and exploitation in the search space, improving convergence speed and adjusting population size for optimal hyperparameter tuning.
The W-Net segmentation method effectively captures contextual information from various scales, accurately identifying skin lesion regions and eliminating noise and hair interference.
The proposed PCSAJSO-CNN outperforms existing techniques such as Inception-ResNet, U-Net, RDCNN, and FLM in terms of accuracy, precision, specificity, and sensitivity.
The method achieved commendable accuracies of 98.15%, 99.38%, 99.70%, 94.68%, and 97.08% on the ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018, ISIC2019, and PH2 datasets, respectively.
The use of PCA combined with the AJSO strategy improves efficiency in finding optimal solutions with minimal resource consumption.

The CNN architecture, optimized by PCSAJSO, possesses a reduced parameter count compared to traditional neural networks, facilitating effective training and avoiding overfitting through dropout layers.
important_remarks: The study claims to use ISIC2016 and ISIC2017 datasets but does not explicitly detail the specific split ratios or class distributions for these two datasets, unlike ISIC2019 (80/10/10 split) and PH2 (200 training instances), raising potential concerns about data leakage or inconsistent evaluation protocols.
The PH2 dataset is extremely small (400 images total), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results for that specific fold.

The paper reports high accuracy (e.g., 99.70% on ISIC2018) but does not provide confidence intervals, standard deviations, or results from multiple random seeds, making it difficult to assess the variance and robustness of the PCSAJSO-CNN model.

No external validation was performed; all models were evaluated on standard public benchmarks (ISIC, PH2) without testing on independent, unseen clinical data from different institutions or demographics.

The comparison with baseline methods (e.g., RDCNN, FLM, Inception-ResNet) is inconsistent; some baselines lack reported metrics for certain datasets (marked as N/A), preventing a complete fair comparison across all models and datasets.

The paper does not report computational complexity, inference time, or parameter counts for the proposed PCSAJSO-CNN compared to the baselines, despite claiming reduced parameters and faster convergence.

The preprocessing steps (Gaussian Filter, Dull Razor) and segmentation (W-Net) are applied before classification, but the paper does not clarify if the segmentation masks were used as input channels or if the classification was performed on the original images post-segmentation, which could influence the reported accuracy.
The optimization algorithm PCSAJSO is a novel contribution, but its specific hyperparameters and the chaotic logistic map details are not fully reproducible from the text provided.
